coredata.dc:title: CDs/ZnO composite material with solid state fluorescence performance for quantitative determination of methyl red content and antibacterial properties
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Mengqi
Zhang, Kaiyue
Ji, Feixiang
Guo, Yurong
Wang, Chao
Wang, Shiping
Chu, Ya
Zhao, Guangjiu
coredata.dc:description: A water-soluble probe composite material (CDs/ZnO) was synthesized by microwave pyrolysis methods of citric acid, urea and zinc oxide (ZnO) with aggregation-induced emission activity. We creatively realized the composite of CDs and nano-ZnO in the form of foam, then used as bactericide for the first time and showed excellent antibacterial properties. Also, the composite materials overcame the problem of aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ) and realized solid-state emission. The materials had two different emission centers, and the two emission peaks were varied with the change of excitation wavelength. Furthermore, Methyl red (MR) showed fluorescence quenching for CDs/ZnO with obvious linear correlation, which demonstrated potential in the measurement of MR in dyeing wastewater. The CDs/ZnO we obtained perfectly inherited the advantages of CDs and nano ZnO, it has not just no cytotoxicity to cells, but also has a good killing effect on bacteria. Hence, this work shows significant adaptable fluorescence-based protocol with enormous potential applications in biology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Composite material
Solid state fluorescence
Antibacterial
Methyl red concentration
Aggregation-induced quenching

Determination of methyl red content in aqueous solution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the CDs/ZnO layer, and the inset is portion of the HRTEM image of CDs/ZnO. (b) XRD pattern of the CDs and CDs/ZnO. (c) XPS spectra of the CDs and CDs/ZnO samples, (c) Surveys, (d) Zn-2p region. (e) FT-IR spectra of the CDs and CDs/ZnO. (f) The UV absorption spectra of CDs and CDs/ZnO samples.
Schematic illustration of the formation process for the CDs/ZnO.

(a) Normalized fluorescence emission spectra of CDs/ZnO powder excited from 320 nm to 500 nm and (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs/ZnO in aqueous solution excited from 320 nm to 500 nm and (c) Normalized fluorescence emission spectra of CDs/ZnO in aqueous solution (d) Chromaticity diagram of CDs / ZnO.

Variation of PL intensity of CDs/ZnO with different concentrations of MR. (a) excited at 440 nm (b) excited at 380 nm and (c) Linear relationship between MR signal intensities and CDs/ZnO with different concentrations.

7 days’ cell growth curves with different concentrations of CDs/ZnO of different cells (a) cells 293 T, (b) cells CHO (c) cells Hela. 24 h cell survival rates of different groups of CDs/ZnO of different cells (d) cells 293 T, (e) cells CHO, (f) cells Hela.
24 h survival rates of different groups (a) E.coli, (b) S.aureus.
C, N, O, Zn content of two kinds of samples.

head.ce:title: CDs/ZnO composite material with solid state fluorescence performance for quantitative determination of methyl red content and antibacterial properties

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: New Sunscreens Development and UV Photoprotection Research Center, Tianjin ChenyinSTI Co., Ltd., 莀茵科创, ChenyinSTI Inc., ChenyinSTI@126.com, Xinghua Road at Xeda, Tianjin 300385, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: New Sunscreens Development and UV Photoprotection Research Center
莀茵科创, ChenyinSTI Inc., ChenyinSTI@126.com

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: New Sunscreens Development and UV Photoprotection Research Center, Tianjin ChenyinSTI Co., Ltd., 莀茵科创, ChenyinSTI Inc., ChenyinSTI@126.com, Xinghua Road at Xeda, Tianjin 300385, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A water-soluble probe composite material (CDs/ZnO) was synthesized by microwave pyrolysis methods of citric acid, urea and zinc oxide (ZnO) with aggregation-induced emission activity. We creatively realized the composite of CDs and nano-ZnO in the form of foam, then used as bactericide for the first time and showed excellent antibacterial properties. Also, the composite materials overcame the problem of aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ) and realized solid-state emission. The materials had two different emission centers, and the two emission peaks were varied with the change of excitation wavelength. Furthermore, Methyl red (MR) showed fluorescence quenching for CDs/ZnO with obvious linear correlation, which demonstrated potential in the measurement of MR in dyeing wastewater. The CDs/ZnO we obtained perfectly inherited the advantages of CDs and nano ZnO, it has not just no cytotoxicity to cells, but also has a good killing effect on bacteria. Hence, this work shows significant adaptable fluorescence-based protocol with enormous potential applications in biology.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent materials have made great progress in the last decade. The most luminescent materials consist of rare-earth-element compounds, quantum dots, and small organic molecules. However, these materials still suffer problems of toxicity, and high cost. Carbon dots (CDs) as emerging nanoparticles have excellent advantages such as facile synthesis, low cost, good biocompatibility, and environmental frendliness [1–4]. It has become one of the most promising next-generation fluorescent material in the future [5–8]. But energy resonance transfer or π-π interaction [9,10] always lead to fluorescence quenching in solid state or high-mass concentration solution. This phenomenon is defined as aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ) [11–14]. ACQ badly confines the practical application of CDs. In recent years, researchers have continuous breakthrough in canceling disadvantages of ACQ and synthesized solid-state fluorescence CDs [15–17]. Zhang Jun and coworkers used two types of eco-friendly cellulose derivatives with positive and negative charges wrap CDs with fibers to effectively suppress aggregation-induced luminescence and the quantum yield reaches up to 71.5% [18]; Qu and coworkers synthesized solid luminescent carbon dots via a one-step hydrothermal method with a quantum yield of 26%, which has been applied in white light-emitting diodes and photoelectric transmission[19]; Zhao and coworkers developed Na ion dispersion (NID) method using sodium citrate as carbon sources and urea as nitrogen dopant to synthesize solid light-emitting CDs. The quantum yield of solid-state N-CDs reached 80.7%, and it was successfully applied to cell imaging [20]. These methods avoided ACQ and realized luminescence at high concentrations and solid state. However, there are still some aspects to be improved, such as increasing quantum yield, seeking new synthesis methods, and expanding excellent applications and so on. In addition, CDs were confirmed with unique biocompatibility and are widely used in biological experiments. Yang and coworkers synthesized high-performance deep red emission CDs by hydrothermal method and explore the deep tissue biological imaging applications [21].

In recent years, with the over-exploitation of resources, bacteria, fungi and other microorganisms have caused great harm to human health. The spread of bacteria leads to the spread of infectious diseases. To overcome this problem, excessive antibiotics are used. The abuse of antibiotics may lead to bio-resistance, and the potential water pollution is also serious problems [22–27]. To make an approach to this problem, looking for green, non-hazardous antibacterial materials has become one of the most hotspots for many material scientists [28,29]. In terms of the most promising luminescent material ZnO, for stable physical and chemical properties, it has become one of the most popular materials in this field [30,31]. The zinc element in nano-ZnO is one of the essential trace elements for the human body, and it has good compatibility with the human body [32,33]. Therefore, the study of antibacterial properties of nano-ZnO is essential and rewarding [34]. Furthermore, there are a large number of oxygen vacant defects in pure ZnO, making it easy to be modified and processed [35,36]. Previous scholars have done a lot of work in the synergic nanomaterials of CDs and ZnO. Ma and coworkers used the CDs and ZnO nanosheets with novel superstructures and successfully synthesized simultaneously from a hydrothermal preparation and thermal decomposition of a porous precursor of metal–organic frameworks [37]. Suzuki and coworkers use of a ZnO sol precursor and CDs, together with a partially fluorinated block copolymer as templating agent to synthesize a porous matrix characterized by interesting energy transfer properties [38]. Alarfaj and coworkers synthesized CDs conjugated ZnO nanocomposite and applied in the detection of Tumor Marker Cytokeratin-19 Fragment (CYFRA 21–1) [39].

Herein, we synthesized novel composite consisting carbon dots (CDs) and ZnO via a one-pot microwave method, which is simple, low-cost and safe to achieve. CDs/ZnO not only has excellent solid state luminescence properties, but also a good solubility in aqueous solution. Morevoer, the good characteristics of luminescence emissions both in the solid state and in aqueous solution shows great potential in methyl red detection. More importantly, the CDs/ZnO we obtained almost inherited the perfect advantages of both CDs and nano ZnO, which is non-cytotoxic to cells, and effectively antibacterial.

Several microscopic techniques have been used to characterize the morphology, structure, surface components and other spectral characteristics. Just as Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were performed from JEOL JEM-2100F Field Transmission Electron Microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by Bruker D8 Focus Diffraction System with Cu Ka radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm). X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained from the Thermo fisher Escalab 250Xi using Al monochromatic Ka radiation (hv = 1486.6 eV), spot size 500 μm as the X-ray source. The binding energies were referenced to the C 1 s line at 284.8 eV from adventitious carbon. The UV spectra of the products in water were surveyed by the OCEAN OPTICS, DH-2000-BAL. The photoluminescence (PL) of the solid powder and aqueous solution was measured by F-380 (GangDong Scientific and Technical Development Co., Ltd, Tianjin), and the exit and entrance slit were set as 5 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was conducted on ALPHA FT-IR spectrophotometer from 370 to 7500 cm−1 using infrared reflection method. A small amount of CDs and CDs/ZnO (about 1 ~ 2 mg each) was added in an agate mortar, and then 200 ~ 400 mg of dry pure potassium bromide (AR grade) was put into the agate mortar.The mixture was stirred evenly, then compressed into a tablet.
Unless otherwise specified, all reagents and compounds were purchased from general reagent companys.

To synthesize the CDs and ZnO (CDs/ZnO): citric acid (AR, Purchased from Heowns) 3.0 g as carbon source, and 3.0 g urea (AR, Aladdin) was used as the nitrogen source with 1.8 g ZnO (Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent), all compounds were mixed with 15 mL H2O, ultrasonically oscillated about 20 minutes. The mixture was heated at medium–high (750 W, Midea, China) for 10 minutes in a microwave oven, then the products of microwave reaction were dissolved in water, centrifuged for 30 minutes, 9000 rads for three times. The supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm pinhole filter membrane, and the filtrate was freeze-dried for 48 hours.

To synthesize the contrast (CDs): citric acid 3.0 g, and 3.0 g urea were used, and all the compounds were mixed with 15 mL H2O, ultrasonically oscillated about 20 minutes. The mixture was heated at medium–high temperature (750 W) for 10 minutes in microwave oven (Midea, China), then the products of microwave reaction were dissolved in water, centrifuged for 30 minutes, 9000 rads for three times. The supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm pinhole filter membrane, dialyzed against a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 48 hours, and the filtrate was freeze-dried for 48 hours.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture, drug treatments and experimental group settings
ce:section.ce:para: In this accordance, low cytotoxicity property is one of the most attractive features of CDs. To assess the cytotoxicity property of CDs/ZnO, human embryonic kidney cells (293 T), human cervical cancer cell lines (Hela) and Chinese Hamster Ovary (CHO) cultured in vitro were treated with different concentrations of CDs/ZnO solutions. Briefly, the above cells were individually cultured in the cellular culture medium as DMEM medium (11965084, Thermo Fisher, Shanghai, China) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, 10091148, Thermo Fisher, Shanghai, China) and 100 U/mL penicillin/streptomycin antibiotics (P/S, 15070063, Thermo Fisher Scientific, China) in 6 well plate (Corning, Beijing, China) at a humidified atmosphere (5% CO2, 95% air and 37 °C). For the cell proliferation abilities analyses, the above cells were individually treated with different concentrations of CDs/ZnO (0, 10, 20, 40, and 80 mg/mL dissolved in DMEM culture medium) for 7 days with cellular proliferation potentials analyzed every 24 h based on the cell numbers. Furthermore, after the treatments of different concentrations of CDs/ZnO (0, 10, 20, 40, and 80 mg/mL dissolved in DMEM medium) for 24 h, the cell survival rates of each group were individually analyzed by Countess II Automated Cell Counters (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Beijing, China).

During the culture of E. coli and S. aureus, different concentrations of CDs/ZnO were supplemented to the culture medium. The control group was not supplemented with any additional CDs/ZnO supplementations (as 0 mg/mL group), while the experimental groups including CDs/ZnO 5 group, CDs/ZnO 10 group, CDs/ZnO 20 group, CDs/ZnO 40 group and CDs/ZnO 80 group were individually supplemented with 5 mg/mL CDs/ZnO, 10 mg/mL CDs/ZnO, 20 mg/mL CDs/ZnO, 40 mg/mL CDs/ZnO and 80 mg/mL CDs/ZnO to the culture medium, respectively. After culture for 24 h, the survival rates of E. coli and S. aureus in different groups were analyzed to detect the antibacterial effect of CDs/ZnO against E. coli and S. aureus. In addition, the survival rates of E. coli and S. aureus in the 0 mg/mL group were set as 100%.

Fig. 1
a shows the TEM image of the CDs/ZnO, and the inset is portion of the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of CDs/ZnO. The spherical nanoparticles illustrated the presence of CDs. The flocculent substance at the back indicate that ZnO mainly exists in the form of nanofoam. The TEM image of ZnO consistent well with the previous work of Li [40] and Xie [41], which existed in the form of foam. Based on the above facts, we assumed that carbon nanodots are dispersed in the ZnO foam matrix, and the density of CDs is not so high. Thus, the ACQ phenomenon of CDs can be effectively inhibited. The average size of CDs is about 2–5 nm, and clear lattice can be seen in the inset. TEM images initially proved the existence of CDs/ZnO. Since then, we have synthesized nanocomposites using a one-pot simple method, but the schematic illustration of the formation process for CDs/ZnO should be decomposed into two stages as illustrated in Fig. 2 .

Fig. 1 b depicts the XRD patterns for CDs and CDs/ZnO samples. For CDs, the broad peak at 22.5° corresponding to the diffraction peak of the graphite (002) crystal plane. From the XRD patterns, we can see two kinds of doping sites: internal CDs and edge CDs, large number of defect sites on the edge, making CDs easy to be modified at the defective edges. While for the CDs/ZnO sample, a similar broad peak centered at 22°, indicating that the CDs were successfully coated. The characteristic diffraction peaks are corresponding to some of the typical sphalerite ZnO phase (PDF#21–1486), suggesting the ZnO foam crystal was synthesized during the microwave process. The distinct contrasts of XRD between the CDs and CDs/ZnO samples provide obvious evidence for the formation of CDs/ZnO. The doping of ZnO leads to the increase of urea content and the decrease of citric acid. To further figure out the structure of CDs and CDs/ZnO, we measured the XPS. Table.1 shows the element content percentage, before and after compositing, the oxygen in the samples increasd from 7.39% to 28.1%, and the nitrogen element slightly increased from 2.48% to 2.65%, Zn reached to 5.1%. Fig. 1 c shows the XPS spectra of the CDs and CDs/ZnO samples, and it revealed the existence of N1s, C1s, and O1s in both samples and Zn2p in CDs/ZnO. In the high-resolution spectrum of Zn 2p of CDs/ZnO in Fig. 1 d, there are two symmetric peaks, one centered at 1023.33 eV corresponds to Zn 2p3/2 and the other centered at 1043.33 eV is assigned to Zn 2p1/2. Moreover, the peak position difference is 23.0 eV, indicating that all zinc elements exist as ZnO [42]. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s in Fig. S2 (a), shows the binding energy of electrons on the 1 s orbital of the carbon atom. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s exhibits four binding energy peaks at 283.36 eV, 285.35 eV, 287.21 eV, 289.46 eV, which are attributed to C–C, C-O/C-N, C = O, C≡N, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s has two peaks at 530.60 eV and 532.77 eV, which are attributed to C-O/ C = O and O–H. The peak around 398.23 eV can be attributed to Amide-N, 399.74 eV attributed to cyano-N, and 401.25 eV to pyridine-N.

In order to further verify the functional group of surface and investigate the electronic interaction, the FT-IR spectra was studied. The FT-IR spectra is shown in Fig. 1 e, the strong and broad peak at 3400 ~ 3100 cm−1 is attributed to the O–H and N–H stretching vibrations. Peaks at 1680 cm−1 –1599 cm−1 were ascribed to the amide I and II bands, respectively. The amide bond (–CONH-) indicates that citric acid and urea undergone in dehydration condensation during reaction. Stretching vibration of –CH2 localized at about 1449 ~ 1400 cm−1. In addition, the stretching vibration peak of ≡C-H were at 668 cm−1. Compared with the CDs, the moderately strong peak at 2218 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C≡N. And the characteristic IR band attributed to the Zn-O stretching is usually found at 440–500 cm−1 [43–44]. Above analyses show that CDs/ZnO has more functional groups containing oxygen and nitrogen in the surface. It is the existence of these hydrophilic functional groups that make our synthesized CDs/ZnO composite material possess a good water solubility. FT-IR and XPS peak splitting results are consistent well.

Fig. 1 f shows the absorption spectra of CDs and CDs/ZnO samples. Contrasting to the broad and low absorption peak of CDs, CDs/ZnO with narrow and strong absorption peak shows better luminous performance. The electron-hole pairs with narrow band gaps are easier to recombine, and narrow and strong absorption can increase light utilization. The sharp and strong absorption peak between 200–300 nm, attributed to the typical π-π* transition of C = C, the absorption peak at 300–400 nm originates from the n-π* transition of C = O and C = N. The low and broad peak in the visible light region 400 ~ 500 nm may be attributed to the absorption of ZnO.

In order to explore the photoluminescence mechanism of CDs/ZnO material, we measured the excitation spectra of the powder and aqueous solution samples at 380 nm, shown in Fig. S1
a, and according to Chua [45], the excitation spectra of Nano-ZnO located at 400 nm, 425 nm and 470 nm. Then the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs/ZnO powder under different excitation wavelengths were measured . Fig. 3
a shows CDs/ZnO powder excitation-dependent emission with the excitation wavelength at 320 ~ 500 nm. As the excitation wavelength changed, there is a significant red shift. When excited at 320 nm, the emission wavelength located at 497 nm, the emission peak wavelength redshifts to 544 nm when excited at 500 nm, and the redshift effect of the PL spectrum is gradually strengthened. The excitation-dependent emission may be due to the rough surface of solid CDs / ZnO and the size is not unified enough. Fig. 3 b shows the Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs/ZnO in aqueous solution excited from 320 nm to 500 nm. An obvious double-peak emission is emerged at 400 nm wavelength excitation, and this feature is not displayed at other wavelengths. Fig. 3 c shows the normalized image of Fig. 3 b. From 497 nm to 530 nm, the fluorescence emission wavelength intensity gradually increased with the excitation wavelength increased. These data shows the CDs/ZnO behaves the excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence emission characteristics. 400 nm was the boundary, when excited at 400 nm, it shows dual wavelength emission. When excited at 320 ~ 380 nm, the emission peak of CDs/ZnO located at around 435 nm, when excited more than 400 nm, the emission peak redshifted to 517 nm. When CDs/ZnO dissolving in water, we think due to the homogeneity and stability of CDs/ZnO solution, both the surface and the carbon core can be excited. Considering the above evidence, it was assumed that the CDs/ZnO composite material may has two different emission centers, and the two emission peaks move with the change of excitation wavelength. We contrasted with the references, and found three emission peaks under excitation wavelength at 360 nm of nano ZnO located at 400 nm, 425 nm, 470 nm, and then measured the emission peak of CD (Fig. S1b), both of them are completely different from CD/ZnO. ZnO completely affects the original emission spectrum of CDs. Considering the above evidence, the CDs/ZnO composite may has two different emission centers, namely surface state and carbon core state. In the solid state, only inhomogeneous surface can be excited, which shows the emission characteristics of excitation wavelength dependence. When CDs/ZnO is dissolved in water, the homogeneous and stable solution is easier to be excited, and both the surface and the carbon core are excited, showing obvious dual wavelength emission characteristics.

We can adjust the excitation wavelength of CDs/ZnO composite material to achieve multi-color luminescence. Moreover, from the absorption spectra and PL spectra, the overlap between the narrow absorption band and PL spectra is small. The weak self-absorption property also contributes to the efficient solid-state luminescence of CDs [46]. According to the emission spectra of the solid state CDs/ZnO composite material, the CDs/ZnO composite material synthesized has the characteristic of excitation wavelength dependence. The chromaticity diagram of CDs/ZnO was obtained in Fig. 3 d. As the excitation wavelength changes, its chromaticity coordinates change accordingly. Therefore, the luminescence can be adjusted by changing the excitation wavelength, and it is applied in the fields of optoelectronic devices for the special optical properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of methyl red content in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para:
To demonstrate the application of the CDs/ZnO we obtained, the experiment of fluorescence decay of methyl red (MR) as a model reaction was carried out. A large amount of textile printing and dyeing wastewater contains complex dyes and compounds, especially azo dyes and their by-products, are carcinogenic and mutagenic [47,48]. Methyl red is a common azo in industry, however, traditional detection methods with complicated procedures, high costs always require professional operations and expensive operating equipment [49–51]. Hence, the high sensitivity, fast response, and easy analysis operation and detection has important application value and significance. The detail of the configuration method was shown in SI. Considered the two emission peaks, all the fluorescence spectra of MR were measured under excitation wavelength of 380 nm and 440 nm, respectively. Fig. 4
a and Fig. 4 b show the PL variation with the different concentrations of MR, and both show obvious fluorescence quenching. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity exhibited an obvious decrease with increasing concentrations of MR at both excited wavelengths, which also confirms the existence of two emission centers of CDs. The fluorescence quenching occurs because of the luminescent group on the surface reacts with MR to form a fluorescent inert substance then reduces the fluorescence intensity. The higher the concentration increased, the weaker the fluorescence intensity was. The above result indicates the two excited wavelengths have the potential to measure MR concentration. We chose the fluorescence at 440 nm, and Fig. 4 c showed a linear decrease over the different concentration of MR in the range from 0 to 5 mg/L, R2 = 0.99844. The above fluorescence quenching curves revealed that our sample has a great value in determining the methyl red content in printing and dye wastewater.

After 24 hours, the cells of different groups were randomly collected and incubated with Gimsa staining solution at 37 ℃ for 5 minutes, followed by cell survival rates analyses. Furthermore, the 7 days’ cell growth curves of different cells in different groups were analyzed according to former studies.

Fig. 5
a-5c gives the 7 days’ cell growth curves of different groups. It can be seen that CDs/ZnO has almost no effect on three kinds of cells in the first 4 days, and all groups increased linearly with the same tendency. After 4 days, the growth curves slightly declined compared with the control group. We hold the view that the cells propagation are therefore not influenced by CDs/ZnO, even at the ultra-high concentration of 80 mg/mL. Fig. 5 d-5f depicts the 24 h cell survival rates of different groups. As it can be seen, the survival rates of 293 T cells intensity slightly decreased with the increase concentration of CDs/ZnO, however, there were almost no toxicity effect of CDs/ZnO on Hela and CHO cells. The previous work revealed the cytotoxicity of nano-ZnO [52,53], and the mechanism is unclear now. The most popular explanation is nano-ZnO induces oxidative stress by generating excessive levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) or by releasing Zn2+ to disrupt cell metabolism and induce cell apoptosis. Obviously, CDs/ZnO exhibited excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity to cells, and perfectly inherit the advantages of CDs. This might attributed to the abundant functional groups (hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, etc.) on the surface of the CDs, which could oxidize ROS or the produced Zn2+. Considered the fact that ZnO nano particles are meant to possess potential applications in medicine such as antimicrobial [54,55], we tested the antibacterial properties of CDs/ZnO.

Fig. 6 represents the 24 h cell survival rates of different groups, and shows that CDs/ZnO has good antibacterial ability against E.coli and S.aureus, and CDs/ZnO are more toxic to E. coli than S.aureus. As the sample concentration increased, both groups of bacterial showed a linear decline in survival rate. As the concentration of CDs/ZnO reached 80 mg/mL, the survival rates of the two groups were 29% and 21%, respectively. Compared to traditional antibiotics, the antibiotics will cause pathogenic microorganisms to develop resistance, making the minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) of antibiotics continue to increase. Long-term discharge of low-dose antibiotics into the environment will increase the resistance of sensitive bacteria. Moreover, resistance genes can expand and evolve in the environment, posing potential threats to the ecological environment and human health. In addition, antibiotics may also be toxic to other organisms. Although, the concentration of our antibacterial materials is high, in the long run, our materials do no harm to the environment and the human body [56]. The elaborated mechanism of ZnO nanoparticles involving in antibacterial activity is still uncertain in a long run. The followed hypothesis may be the reasons for antimicrobial activity that nanoparticles of different sizes aggregate at a certain concentration to form agglomerates of similar size, and then have the antibacterial activity. In addition, nano-ZnO has not only sterilization activity under ultraviolet radiation, but also antimicrobial activity under dark conditions [57–58]. This conjecture is also consistent with our experimental results.

Taking it together, CDs/ZnO obtained in our study, can kill the bacteria in a targeted manner without affecting living mammalian cells, which perfectly inherited the advantages of CDs and ZnO. It can be widely applied as a disinfectant in our daily life, more importantly, it is non-expensive and easy to prepare. Our work provides the idea for the research of new environmentally friendly bacterial disinfectants.

In summary, the CDs/ZnO can be successfully synthesized by microwave pyrolysis methods using citric acid, urea and ZnO. On the basis of CDs, ZnO is combined in the form of nano foam, which overcomes the problem of ACQ and realizes solid state emission. In addition, the aqueous solution of the composite with narrow absorption peak and almost no overlap between absorption and emission shows re-absoption free fluorescence properties. The results reflect the luminescent material exhibits completely different luminescent phenomena in solid state and solution. The solid state shows wavelength-dependent luminescence due to its uneven surface, while the solution shows dual wavelength emission, and this is attributed to the excitation of both carbon nucleus and surface state in solution

The CDs/ZnO composite was a water-soluble fluorescent probe, which can quench the fluorescence of MR in textile and printing wastewater. The visual detection platform for MR fluorescence was constructed in this work. We used the synthesized composite aqueous solution to realize the fluorescence detection of MR with good linearity. It makes up for the shortcomings of high cost and complicated operation of traditional MR measurement, and realizes a water-soluble probe with simple operation, high sensitivity and low cost.

Moreover, we tested the cell survival rate and cell proliferation with different concentrations of CDs/ZnO of different cells including 293 T, CHO and Hela cells. The CDs/ZnO exhibited excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity to these cells. Afterwards, the antibacterial properties of CDs/ZnO were also analyzed, and found that the sample has good antibacterial ability against E.coli and S.aureus. All in all, the CDs/ZnO we obtained almost inherited the perfect advantages of CDs and nano ZnO, including non-cytotoxicity to cells, and good killing effect on bacteria. Therefore, our work showed significant adaptable fluorescence-based protocol with enormous potential applications in biology. The CDs/ZnO plays a role of potential candidate for new non-toxic fungicides which will attract more attention in the near future.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21873068, 21573229, and 21422309). G.J.Z. also thanks the financial support from the Frontier Science Project of the Knowledge Innovation Program of Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), Project for Excellent Member of CAS Youth Innovation Promotion Association, the Open Research Funds of State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics (Southeast University) and State Key Laboratory of Magnetic Resonance and Atomic and Molecular Physics (Wuhan Institute of Physics and Mathematics of Chinese Academy of Sciences), and Double First-Rate and Peiyang Scholar Projects of Tianjin University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2021.08.021.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-infrared excitation/emission and multiphoton-induced fluorescence of carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Li, P. Jing, L. Sun, Y. An, X. Shan, X. Lu, D. Zhou, D. Han, D. Shen, Y. Zhai, S. Qu, R, Zbořil, A. L. Rogach, Near-infrared excitation/emission and multiphoton-induced fluorescence of carbon dots, Adv. Mater. 30, (2018) 1705913.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots-in-matrix boosting intriguing luminescence properties and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Li, B. Wang, H. Zhang, J. Yu, Carbon dots-in-matrix boosting intriguing luminescence properties and applications, Small, 15 (2019) 1805504.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanozymes: classification, catalytic mechanisms, activity regulation, and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Huang, J. Ren, X. Qu, Nanozymes: classification, catalytic mechanisms, activity regulation, and applications, Chem. Rev. 119 (2019) 4357 – 4412.
